questions.question_text: How many types of seller disclosure forms does Delaware require for residential property?
distractors: One universal form
Two different forms
Four different forms
questions.explanation: The Delaware Real Estate Commission publishes three Seller's Disclosure of Real Property Condition Report forms: the standard residential form (used for 1-4 family dwellings including condos), New Construction Only, and Vacant Land. A separate Exemption Property Certification covers transactions exempt under 6 Del. C. §2577, such as sheriff's/foreclosure sales, court-ordered transfers, and transfers to a spouse or lineal relatives.

questions.question_text: A home is sold at a sheriff's foreclosure sale for default on a mortgage. Must a Delaware seller disclosure form be provided?
distractors: Yes, all residential sales require one
Only if the price exceeds $100,000
Only if the buyer's agent requests it
questions.explanation: Court-ordered transfers, sheriff's sales, and deeds in lieu of foreclosure to the mortgagee are exempt from Delaware's seller disclosure requirements (6 Del. C. §2577(2)-(4)); the Exemption Property Certification is used instead. Note: once a bank has taken title and later resells the property itself, that resale is NOT exempt — the exemption covers the sheriff's sale and the transfer to the mortgagee, not the bank's subsequent sale.

questions.question_text: A seller is transferring a condo to their daughter as a gift. Is a disclosure form required?
distractors: Yes, all condo sales need disclosure
Only if the condo is occupied
Only if a licensee is involved
questions.explanation: Transfers to a spouse or to persons in the lineal line of consanguinity are exempt (6 Del. C. §2577(7)). Other exemptions include court-ordered transfers, sheriff's sales, deeds in lieu of foreclosure to the mortgagee, fiduciary transfers, co-owner transfers, divorce-related spousal transfers, and transfers to or from government entities. New construction is NOT exempt — builders complete the New-Construction version of the disclosure form.

questions.question_text: A builder sells a newly constructed home that has never been occupied. Which disclosure requirement applies?
questions.correct_answer: The New Construction disclosure form is required
distractors: Exempt from all seller disclosure forms
Standard resale disclosure form required
Vacant land form required
questions.explanation: New construction is not exempt from Delaware's Buyer Property Protection Act. The builder-seller must complete the DREC 'Real Property Condition Report — New Construction Only' form, which includes the statutory fire-sprinkler-option notice (6 Del. C. §2578(b)) and street-repaving disclosure, and deliver it to prospective buyers before an offer is made (§2573). Radon disclosure under §2572A also applies.

questions.question_text: A seller owns a vacant lot and a condo unit, selling both. How many disclosure forms are needed?
questions.correct_answer: Two forms, one for each property
distractors: One combined form covers both
Three forms plus a certification
None if sold as a package deal
questions.explanation: Each property requires its own form: the vacant land transfer uses the Vacant Land disclosure form, and the condo (as a residential dwelling unit) uses the standard Real Property Condition Report. Each disclosure report is property-specific — it identifies a single property address and becomes part of that property's agreement of sale (6 Del. C. §2573) — so each parcel needs its own form.

questions.question_text: What is Delaware's requirement regarding radon in residential real estate transactions?
questions.correct_answer: Sellers must disclose known test results
distractors: Sellers must test before listing
Buyers must test before closing
Radon disclosure is voluntary only
questions.explanation: Delaware requires sellers to disclose any known radon test results for residential properties. The law does not mandate that sellers conduct testing, but any existing results must be shared with buyers.

questions.question_text: A seller tested for radon five years ago with elevated results but says the report is lost. What must the seller disclose?
distractors: Nothing if the report is unavailable
Only that testing was performed
Results only if buyer specifically asks
questions.explanation: The seller has knowledge of elevated radon test results and must disclose that information regardless of whether the physical report is available. Known results must be disclosed even if documentation has been lost.

questions.question_text: Why does Delaware require radon disclosure in residential transactions?
questions.correct_answer: Radon is the second leading cause of lung cancer
distractors: Radon causes structural foundation damage
Federal law mandates state disclosure
Radon affects property resale values
questions.explanation: Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas and the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking. Delaware's disclosure requirement protects buyers by ensuring they are informed about known radon levels in the home.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks the listing agent whether radon testing has been done. The agent is unsure. What is the best course of action?
questions.correct_answer: Ask the seller and relay accurate info
distractors: Tell the buyer radon is not an issue
Suggest the buyer hire an inspector
State that radon testing is not required
questions.explanation: The agent should inquire with the seller about any known radon test results and relay accurate information. Agents must not misrepresent or guess about radon status. While buyers can also test independently, the agent has a duty to help obtain available information.

questions.question_text: Which statement about radon disclosure exemptions in Delaware is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Certain property types may be exempt
distractors: No exemptions exist for any property
All commercial properties must disclose
Only condos above ground floor are exempt
questions.explanation: Radon disclosure applies only to residential real property on which a dwelling exists (6 Del. C. §2572A(a)-(b)) — vacant land and non-residential property are outside the requirement — and transfers exempt under §2577 (sheriff's sales, family transfers, etc.) are also excluded, using the Exempt Property Certification instead.

questions.question_text: Under Delaware law, must a listing agent voluntarily disclose that someone died in the home?
questions.correct_answer: No duty to disclose deaths in property
distractors: All deaths within the past five years
Only homicides or violent deaths
Any death the agent knows about
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2927, the fact or suspicion that property was the site of a homicide, suicide, or other felony (except arson) is not a material fact, and no cause of action lies for failing to disclose it. However, if a buyer makes a specific written request, the owner or licensee must answer truthfully (§2927(c)) — except questions about HIV/AIDS status of occupants, which may never be answered (§2927(d)).

questions.question_text: A buyer directly asks the listing agent whether a murder occurred in the home. The agent knows one did. What should the agent do?
distractors: Deny it since there's no duty to disclose
Disclose the full police report details
Refer the buyer to public crime records
questions.explanation: There is no duty to volunteer that a property is psychologically impacted, but a licensee may never misrepresent. If the buyer makes a specific written request, 24 Del. C. §2927(c) requires the owner, landlord, or licensee to answer truthfully to the best of their knowledge; declining to answer is only an option for oral inquiries. The one absolute bar: psychological-impact questions about HIV/AIDS or similar diseases may not be answered even if asked (§2927(d)).

questions.question_text: A developer is selling lots in a new subdivision located within 300 feet of a Delaware Agricultural Preservation District. What notice obligation applies?
questions.correct_answer: A prescribed agricultural-district notice in the deed restrictions and agreements of sale
distractors: No notice is ever required near farmland
A verbal warning about farm odors at closing
Notice only if the farm files a complaint
questions.explanation: Under 3 Del. C. §910(a)(1), the owner of any new subdivision development located wholly or partly within 300 feet of an Agricultural Preservation District must include a statutory notice in the deed restrictions and any leases or agreements of sale, warning that normal agricultural activities (noise, dust, manure and other odors, agricultural chemicals, nighttime operations) have priority status. For ordinary resales, a seller must still disclose known nuisances as material information on the disclosure report, but Delaware imposes no general farm-proximity disclosure.

questions.question_text: A new homeowner near a farm sues over manure odors. How does Delaware's Right to Farm Act affect the claim?
questions.correct_answer: It limits nuisance claims against farms
distractors: It guarantees compensation for odors
It requires the farm to reduce emissions
It has no effect on residential complaints
questions.explanation: Delaware's Right to Farm Act protects established agricultural operations from nuisance lawsuits by neighboring property owners who moved in after the farm was established. This law limits the homeowner's ability to bring a successful nuisance claim.

questions.question_text: An agent is asked to disclose whether paranormal activity has been reported in a listing. What does Delaware law require?
questions.correct_answer: No duty to disclose paranormal claims
distractors: Must disclose if documented in writing
Must disclose all known property history
Must disclose if it affects property value
questions.explanation: Delaware's psychologically-impacted-property statute (24 Del. C. §§2902, 2927) covers homicide, suicide, other felonies (except arson), and occupant HIV/AIDS status — it does not mention paranormal claims. No Delaware law makes alleged paranormal activity a material fact, so there is no duty to disclose it, and §2936(c)(6) bars volunteering stigmatizing suspicions without consent. As always, a licensee must not affirmatively misrepresent if asked.
